author: Augustine of Hippo
book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.

ccel.title: NPNF1-07. St. Augustine: Homilies on the Gospel of John; Homilies on the First Epistle of John; Soliloquies

title: Chapter XIX. 1–16.

source.title: NPNF1-07. St. Augustine: Homilies on the Gospel of John; Homilies on the First Epistle of John; Soliloquies

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: On the Jews crying out that they did not wish Jesus to be released unto them at the passover, but Barabbas the robber; not the Saviour, but the murderer; not the Giver of life, but the destroyer,—“then Pilate took Jesus and scourged Him.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: We must believe that Pilate acted thus for no other reason than that the Jews, glutted with the injuries done to Him, might consider themselves satisfied, and desist from madly pursuing Him even unto death.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: With a similar intention was it that, as governor, he also permitted his cohort to do what follows, or even perhaps ordered them, although the evangelist is silent on the subject.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: For he tells us what the soldiers did thereafter, but not that Pilate ordered it.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: “And the soldiers,” he says, “platted a crown of thorns, and put it on His head, and they clothed Him with a purple robe.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: And they came to Him and said, Hail, King of the Jews!

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: And they smote Him with their hands.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Thus were fulfilled the very things which Christ had foretold of Himself; thus were the martyrs moulded for the endurance of all that their persecutors should be pleased to inflict; thus, by concealing for a time the terror of His power, He commended to us the prior imitation of His patience; thus the kingdom which was not of this world overcame that proud world, not by the ferocity of fighting, but by the humility of suffering; and thus the grain of corn that was yet to be multiplied was sown amid the horrors of shame, that it might come to fruition amid the wonders of glory.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: “Pilate went forth again, and saith unto them, Behold, I bring him forth, that ye may know that I find no fault in him.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Then came Jesus forth, wearing the crown of thorns and the purple robe.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: And he saith unto them, Behold the man!”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Hence it is apparent that these things were done by the soldiers not without Pilate’s knowledge, whether it was that he ordered them or only permitted them, namely, for the reason we have stated above, that His enemies might all the more willingly drink in the sight of such derisive treatment, and cease to thirst further for His blood.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Jesus goes forth to them wearing the crown of thorns and the purple robe, not resplendent in kingly power, but laden with reproach; and the words are addressed to them, Behold the man!

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: If you hate your king, spare him now when you see him sunk so low; he has been scourged, crowned with thorns, clothed with the garments of derision, jeered at with the bitterest insults, struck with the open hand; his ignominy is at the boiling point, let your ill-will sink to zero.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: But there is no such cooling on the part of the latter, but rather a further increase of heat and vehemence.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: “When the chief priests, therefore, and attendants saw Him, they cried out, saying, Crucify, crucify him.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Pilate saith unto them Take ye him and crucify him; for I find no fault in him.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: The Jews answered him, We have a law, and by the law he ought to die because he made himself the Son of God.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Behold another and still greater ground of hatred.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: The former, indeed, seemed but a small matter, as that shown towards the usurpation, by an unlawful act of daring, of the royal power; and yet of neither did Jesus falsely claim possession, but each of them is truly His as both the only-begotten Son of God, and by Him appointed King upon His holy hill of Zion; and both might He now have shown to be His, were it not that in proportion to the greatness of His power, He preferred to manifest the corresponding greatness of His patience.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: “When Pilate, therefore, heard that saying, he was the more afraid; and entered again into the judgment hall, and saith unto Jesus, Whence art thou?

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: But Jesus gave him no answer.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: It is found, in comparing the narratives of all the evangelists, that this silence on the part of our Lord Jesus Christ took place more than once, both before the chief priests and before Herod, to whom, as Luke intimates, Pilate had sent Him for a hearing, and before Pilate himself; so that it was not in vain that the prophecy regarding Him had preceded, “As the lamb before its shearer was dumb, so He opened not His mouth,” especially on those occasions when He answered not His questioners.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: For although He frequently replied to questions addressed to Him, yet because of those in regard to which He declined making any reply, the metaphor of the lamb is supplied, in order that in His silence He might be accounted not as guilty, but innocent.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: When, therefore, He was passing through the process of judgment, wherever He opened not His mouth it was in the character of a lamb that He did so; that is, not as one with an evil conscience who was convicted of his sins, but as one who in His meekness was sacrificed for the sins of others.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: “Then saith Pilate unto Him, Speakest thou not unto me? knowest thou not that I have power to crucify thee, and have power to release thee?

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Jesus answered: Thou wouldest have no power against me, except it were given thee from above: therefore he that delivered me unto thee hath the greater sin.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Here, you see, He replied; and yet wherever He replied not, it is not as one who is criminal or cunning, but as a lamb; that is, in simplicity and innocence He opened not His mouth.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Accordingly, where He made no answer, He was silent as a sheep; where He answered, He taught as the Shepherd.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Let us therefore set ourselves to learn what He said, what He taught also by the apostle, that “there is no power but of God;” and that he is a greater sinner who maliciously delivereth up to the power the innocent to be slain, than the power itself, if it slay him through fear of another power that is greater still.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Of such a sort, indeed, was the power which God had given to Pilate, that he should also be under the power of Cæsar.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Wherefore “thou wouldest have,” He says, “no power against me,” that is, even the little measure thou really hast, “except” this very measure, whatever its amount, “were given thee from above.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: But knowing as I do its amount, for it is not so great as to render thee altogether independent, “therefore he that delivered me unto thee hath the greater sin.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: He, indeed, delivered me to thy power at the bidding of envy, whilst thou art to exercise thy power upon me through the impulse of fear.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: And yet not even through the impulse of fear ought one man to slay another, especially the innocent; nevertheless to do so by an officious zeal is a much greater evil than under the constraint of fear.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: And therefore the truth-speaking Teacher saith not, “He that delivered me to thee,” he only hath sin, as if the other had none; but He saith, “hath the greater sin,” letting him understand that he himself was not exempt from blame.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: For that of the latter is not reduced to nothing because the other is greater.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: “Hence Pilate sought to release Him.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: What is to be understood by the word here used, “hence,” as if he had not been seeking to do so before?

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Read what precedes, and thou wilt find that he had already for some time been seeking to release Jesus.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: By the original word, therefore, we are to understand, on this account, that is, for this reason, that he might not contract sin by slaying an innocent man who had been delivered into his hands, even though his sin would be less than that of the Jews, who delivered Him to him to be put to death.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: “From thence,” therefore, that is, for this reason, that he might not commit such a sin, “he sought” not now for the first time, but from the beginning, “to release Him.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: “But the Jews cried out, saying, If thou let this man go, thou art not Cæsar’s friend: whosoever maketh himself a king, speaketh against Cæsar.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: They thought to inspire Pilate with greater fear by terrifying him about Cæsar, in order that he might put Christ to death, than formerly when they said, “We have the law, and by the law he ought to die, because he made himself the Son of God.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: It was not their law, indeed, that impelled him through fear to the deed of murder, but rather it was his fear of the Son of God that held him back from the crime.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: But now he could not set Cæsar, who was the author of his own power, at nought, in the same way as the law of another nation.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: As yet, however, the evangelist proceeds to say: “But when Pilate heard these sayings, he brought Jesus forth, and sat down before the tribunal, in a place that is called the Pavement, but in the Hebrew, Gabbatha.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: And it was the preparation of the passover, and about the sixth hour.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: The question, at what hour the Lord was crucified, because of the testimony supplied by another evangelist, who says, “And it was the third hour, and they crucified Him,” we shall consider as we can, if the Lord please, when we are come to the passage itself where His crucifixion is recorded.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: When Pilate, therefore, had sat down before the tribunal, “he saith unto the Jews, Behold your king!

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: But they cried out, Away with him, away with him, crucify him.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Pilate said unto them, Shall I crucify your king?”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: As yet he tries to overcome the terror with which they had inspired him about Cæsar, by seeking to break them from their purpose on the ground of the ignominy it brought on themselves, with the words, “Shall I crucify your king?” when he failed to soften them on the ground of the ignominy done to Christ; but by and by he is overcome by fear.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: For “the chief priests answered, We have no king but Cæsar.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Then delivered he Him therefore unto them to be crucified.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: For he would have every appearance of acting against Cæsar if, on their declaration that they had no king but Cæsar, he were wishing to impose on them another king by releasing without punishment one whom for these very attempts they had delivered unto him to be put to death.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: “Therefore he delivered Him unto them to be crucified.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: But was it, then, anything different that he had previously desired when he said, “Take ye him, and crucify him;” or even earlier still, “Take ye him, and judge him according to your law?”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: And why did they show so great reluctance, when they said, “It is not lawful for us to put any man to death,” and were in every way urgent to have Him slain not by themselves, but by the governor, and therefore refused to receive Him for the purpose of putting Him to death, if now for the same purpose they actually do receive Him?

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Or if such be not the case, why was it said, “Then delivered he Him therefore unto them to be crucified?”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Or is it of any importance?

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Plainly it is.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: For it was not said, “Then delivered he Him therefore unto them” that they might crucify Him, but “that He might be crucified,” that is, that He might be crucified by the judicial sentence and power of the governor.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: But it is for this reason that the evangelist has said that He was delivered to them, that he might show that they were implicated in the crime from which they tried to hold themselves aloof; for Pilate would have done no such thing, save to implement what he perceived to be their fixed desire.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: The words, however, that follow, “And they took Jesus, and led Him away,” may now refer to the soldiers, the attendants of the governor.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: For it is more clearly stated afterwards, “When the soldiers therefore had crucified Him,” although the evangelist properly does so even when he attributes the whole to the Jews, for they it was that received what they had with the utmost greediness demanded, and they it was that did all that they compelled to be done.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: But the events that follow must be made the subject of consideration in another discourse.
